questions.question: Why use FEC instead of retransmission (ARQ) for real-time media?
options: Retransmission is too slow: by the time the retransmitted packet arrives, its playout deadline has passed
ARQ does not work over UDP
questions.explanation: In real-time media, the playout deadline is tight. FEC sends redundant data proactively so the receiver can repair losses immediately without waiting for a round-trip retransmission.

questions.question: Why does XOR-based FEC fail to repair burst loss?
options: A burst often hits 2+ packets in the same FEC group, but one parity can only repair one loss
Burst loss corrupts the parity packet
FEC only works on random loss
questions.explanation: XOR parity can repair exactly one lost packet per group. A burst loss of 2+ consecutive packets likely falls in the same FEC group, exceeding the single-loss repair capacity.

questions.question: How does interleaving help FEC handle burst loss?
options: It spreads consecutive packets across different FEC groups, so a burst hits different groups as single losses
questions.explanation: Interleaving reorders packets so consecutive media frames are in different FEC groups. A burst loss of 3 transmission slots becomes 3 single losses in 3 different groups, each repairable by FEC.

questions.question: What is the bandwidth overhead of XOR FEC with group size k=4?
questions.explanation: With k=4, one parity packet is sent for every 4 media packets: 5 total for 4 original, which is 1/4 = 25% overhead.
